topic: bushfire season anxiety — smoke in Melbourne

turns.content: the sky is orange and i'm having a normal one
Smoke haze? Where are you located?

melbourne. there's fires up in the northeast and the wind has just pushed all the smoke down here. i woke up this morning and it literally looked like mars outside my window. i could taste it in the air when i walked to the tram stop
That sounds really unpleasant. Bushfire smoke in the city is rough — especially when you can taste it. Are you staying indoors as much as possible?

trying to but i had a shift at the cafe this arvo and we have outdoor seating so i was basically just serving coffee in a smoke cloud. my eyes were stinging so bad. and the customers were sitting out there like it was fine??? some guy was eating a croissant outside in literal hazardous air quality like sir your lungs are not going to thank you
People are weirdly nonchalant about smoke haze. It's genuinely bad for you though — especially extended exposure. Did your boss not consider closing the outdoor section?

lol no. capitalism doesn't stop for particulate matter apparently. and the thing that gets me is like... this is going to be EVERY summer now. this isn't a freak event. this is just what december looks like in australia now. fire season starts earlier every year and lasts longer and everyone just goes 'oh yeah bit smoky today' like it's weather and not a symptom of the entire planet breaking

You're not wrong that fire seasons have been getting longer and more intense. It's understandable to feel frustrated when something that significant gets treated as just background noise. The normalization of it is its own kind of problem.

exactly!! the normalization!!! we had literal black summer in 2019-2020 which was genuinely apocalyptic and now we just... accept that summer means smoke? i study environmental science i KNOW the data on this and it makes it worse because i can't just be blissfully ignorant. i see the projections and they're not great

That's a real burden of knowledge — studying exactly the systems that are driving these events means you can't look away from the implications. Do you find that studying it helps you feel more agency, or does it mostly just amplify the anxiety?

genuinely both at the same time which is a fun emotional place to be lol. like on one hand i feel like at least i'm doing something by researching it. on the other hand i know enough to know that individual action is basically meaningless without systemic change and systemic change is happening at the speed of a dead snail. so. love that for us

The "informed helplessness" is real. For what it's worth, research does contribute to systemic change — it's just a slower pipeline than anyone wants. The data you and others produce is what eventually forces policy shifts.

yeah i know i know. it just doesn't feel like enough when the sky is literally orange outside my window right now and my eczema is flaring up because the smoke is making my skin absolutely freak out. anyway sorry for the doom spiral lol i just needed to yell into the void about it

No need to apologize — it's a completely valid thing to be upset about. Take care of yourself tonight, especially with the skin irritation from the smoke. Stay inside if you can, keep windows closed, and maybe give yourself permission to just feel frustrated without trying to solve all of climate change before bed.

ha. ok fair. i will simply not solve climate change tonight. revolutionary concept. thanks for listening to my unhinged climate rant... i reckon i'll go close my window and put on some moisturizer and pretend the sky is a normal colour
Solid plan. Moisturizer and denial — the Australian summer survival kit. Hope the smoke clears soon. Take care.
